ai_strategy_lead.name: AI at the Graduate Center / Teach@CUNY AI Toolkit
ai_strategy_lead.title: Public AI strategy or initiative lead / hub identified from cited sources

ai_strategy.summary: CUNY Graduate School and University Center has a public AI posture anchored in AI at the Graduate Center / Teach@CUNY AI Toolkit. The cited sources show activity across responsible-use guidance, teaching resources, research or infrastructure, and AI literacy or workforce preparation.

key_provisions: Use AI transparently and follow course, research, workplace and academic-integrity expectations.
Do not enter confidential, regulated, sensitive, unpublished or protected institutional data into unapproved public AI tools.
Human users remain accountable for accuracy, bias, copyright, privacy and final decisions based on AI output.
ai_policy.summary: CUNY Graduate School and University Center publishes AI policy or guidance with a permissive stance: AI use is allowed or actively explored, but bounded by privacy, ethics, academic integrity, compliance and human-review expectations.

governance_structure.summary: AI governance at CUNY Graduate School and University Center is evidenced through AI at the Graduate Center / Teach@CUNY AI Toolkit and the cited policy or strategy sources. Public sources did not identify a separate chief AI officer during this sweep.

tools_and_vendors.name: Google.org AI literacy funding

initiatives.policy_implications: Moves AI literacy from isolated course policy toward measured curriculum and assessment frameworks.
initiatives.title: Empire AI student AI literacy assessment expansion
initiatives.description: A January 2026 Google.org grant of more than $1 million expands Graduate Center-led Empire AI research into how students across degree levels and disciplines can learn with AI. The project includes surveys, focus groups, interviews, and collection and assessment of syllabi, assignments, and teaching artifacts involving thousands of students and faculty.
initiatives.significance: This gives a public R1/system consortium model for measuring AI literacy interventions at scale rather than only issuing guidance.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Critical AI Literacy at CUNY student voice research
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: The Graduate Center grant explicitly seeks voices from thousands of students across degree types and disciplines, including those questioning or resisting AI, to shape AI literacy pedagogy.

regulatory_context.summary: CUNY Graduate School and University Center operates within a state or system AI-policy context cited in this profile.

narrative_summary: CUNY Graduate School and University Center now shows a more measurable AI-literacy posture: Graduate Center-led Google.org grants support a three-year critical AI literacy curriculum project and a 2026 Empire AI expansion that studies thousands of students and faculty across degree levels, disciplines, syllabi, assignments, and focus groups. The profile still records responsible-use guidance and research capacity, while adding CUNY system funding through a $3 million AI Innovation Fund for 113 campus-led projects. Public evidence is strongest for curriculum, assessment, and equity-oriented student voice research; precise enterprise GenAI user counts, full Empire AI compute allocations for the Graduate Center, and student outcome results remain undisclosed.

gaps_acknowledged: No standalone CUNY Graduate Center chief AI officer role was identified in public sources.
Empire AI/Graduate Center public pages do not disclose exact compute allocation, GPU entitlement, or active enterprise AI platform user counts for Graduate Center users.
The 2026 Google.org expansion documents planned and ongoing student/faculty assessment, but public results and subgroup outcome metrics are not yet published.
